content.full_text:
Re:
Fern PolrrrcAl- Pnacrrcps C oMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 e Fax (916) 322-0886
March 1t,2009
Frank O'Connell
462 Webster Street, Suite 2
Monterey, CA93940
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-031
Dear Mr. O'Connell:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented;
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact
when it renders advice. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, please note that our
advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the
application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as corlmon law conflict of interest or
Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTTONS
1. As an elected official on the Marina City Council and licensed attorney in the State of
California, would you have a disqualifying conflict of interest by receiving payment from a
client in the amount of $500 for legal services if the council is presented with matters relating to
your client's park?
2. May you retum or refund the $500 paid by your client, or a portion of it, in order to
prevent disqualification?
CONCLUSIONS
1. The payments you received from your client on January L4,2009, is an economic
interest if it was received within the l2-month period preceding the government decision. (See
I The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8 I l() through 18997 of Title 2 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


Our File No. 4-09-031
Page-2
Section 87103(c).) Therefore, you will not be able to participate in any decisions that will have a
foreseeable and material financial effect on your client.
2. There is no statutory exception that allows for the reimbursement of income once
received in order to avoid a conflict of interest. Therefore, retuming all or a portion of the
payment made by your client will not prevent you from being disqualified *h"n matters
regarding your client's mobile home park come before the city council.
FACTS
You were elected to the Marina City Council on Novemb er 4,20O8 and presently serve in
this capacity. You are also a licensed attorney in California. Prior to the election, you provided
legal services to a client who owns real property in Marina including a mobile horne park. you
received payment for these legal services in the amount of $500 on May 15, 200g. On
January 14,2009, you received a second payment of $500 from the same client for legal
services. You anticipate the city council will be deciding matters involving several mobile home
parks in the city, including your client's mobile home park.
POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST
Step One: Are you a "public official, pursuant to the Act?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials.', (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bxl).) A "public official" is ;every member, ofiicer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency." (Section 82048.) You currently sit o, tt "
Marina City Council, thus qualifying as a public official within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision," when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints a person, oblig-ates or
commits his agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) As a member of the city council, lou will be
participating in decisions relating to several mobile home parks in the city, including your
client's mobile home.park. Therefore you will be participating in makinga governmental
decision as specified in the Act. z
2 A city councilmember is a public official who holds office pursuant to Section 872pt,and therefore is
subject to certain procedures concerning the manner of disqualification. (Section 87 I 05; Regulations lg7o2.l and
18702.5) If you have a conflict of interest in a decision to be considered at a noticed public-meeting, then you must:
( l) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, publicly identify on rhe record of tire meeting eaih type-of
economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest as discussei in reguiation
18702.5(bX I XB): recuse yourself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item
(Regulation 18702.5(b).) Note that Regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (d)( l) and (d)(3) provide exceptions tbr
matters on consent calendars and for speaking as a member of the public regarding an applicable personal interest.


Our File No. 4-09-031
Page-3
Step Three: What are your "economic interests?,,
A public official has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87103 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family,
or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
1. An economic interest in any business entity in which the public official has a
direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more; (Section g7103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in any business entity in which the public official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any posititn of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703. t(b).)
2. An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 19703.2.)
3. An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
18703(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "[ncome" includes a pro rata share of any income
of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, dirlctly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section 82030(a).j
4. An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section AZf O:[JX
Regulation 18703.4.)
5. An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 19703.5.)
Your letter identifies the following economic interests:
Business Entity:
You have an economic interest in your law practice as a business entity because you
presumably have an investment of $2,000 or more in your law practice. (Sect-ion 87103ia);
Regulation 18703.1(a).) Additionally, you have an economic interest in your law practice as a
business entity because,You hold a position of management pursuant to Section gZlOf(a) ana
Regulation I 8703. I (b).'
Source of Income:
"[ncome" is defined as "a payment received, including but limited to any salary, wage,
advance, dividend, interest, proceeds from any sale, gift, including any gift of food o. t"urr:og",
loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by the filer-, reirnbursement for expensles,
3Income of an individual also includes a.pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in
which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficiall-y a l0-percent interest or greater. We assume
your legal practice is a sole proprietorship therefore, you own a l0-percent inierest or greater.


Our File No. 4-09-031
Page-4
per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an
employer, and including any community property interest in the income oi a spouse. (Section
82030.)
From the facts presented, you have received two payments from the same client in the
past year. The first payment was made on May 15, 2008 for $500 for legal services involving
the eviction of your client's tenant. The second payment was received on January 14,2OO9 for
similar legal services. These payments are considered "income" for purposes of itre Act if they
are received "within 12 months prior to the time when the [governmental] decision is made."
(Section 87103(c).) Therefore, your client is a source of income under the Act for l2months
after $500 was received from the client.
Your request for advice specifically asks whether you can refund a portion or all of your
client's payment in order to avoid disqualification. As stated above, ",.ro"nily there is no
provision in the Act that permits refunding of a payment after a payment has-been received to
avoid disqualification. Therefore, your client will be considered a source of income to you.
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests, therefore, for
purposes of this letter, we assume that you have no other economic interests relevant to the
decision you have identified.
Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a given economic
interest is material, if it is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental deiision.
Regulation 18704(a).
Source of Income and Business Entity: Regulation 18704.1(a) provides the rule for
determining whether or not a business entity or a source of income is directly or indirectly
involved in a decision:
"A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of
gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that
person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
\ 
"(2) is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or official's agency. A person islhe subject;f a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with,
the subject person."
The governmental decision about which you have inquired relates to matters coming
before the council involving several mobile home parks in the city which includes your client's
mobile home park. Based on the facts presented, your client is directly involved in the


Our File No. 4-09-031
Page-5
govemmental decision, pursuant to Regulation 18704.1(a)(2) because your client is ..a named
party" in the proceeding or is "the subject of' the proceeding in the decision before the city
council.
Conversely, your law practice is indirectly involved in the govemmental decision because
it did not initiate the proceeding and is not a named party or is the iubject of the proceeding.
Steps Five and Six: Witl there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
economic interests?
Materialitv
Client
Regulation 18705.3 sets the materiality standard for economic interests in persons who
are sources of income. Regulation 18705.3(a) provides: "any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official, and who is directly involved
before the official's agency, is deemed material." Pursuant to Regulation 18705.3, the effect of
the council's decision on your client would be deemed material because the govemmental
decision would presumably have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect.
Law Practice
If a business entity is not direct involved in a govemmental decision, the materiality
standard of Regulation 18705.1(c), applies. Subdivision (cX4) applies to small business entities
that are not publically traded. Pursuant to this materiality standard, the financial effect of a
governmental decision on a small business (in this case, your law practice) is material if it is
reasonabl y foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000
or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating exisiing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o, mo.i; o,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,'
(Regulation I 8705. I (cXa).)
Foreseeabilitv
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Sdether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made
depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be certain to be
considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a


Our File No. 4-09-031
Page-6
decision is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (/lr re Thorner,
supra, at 198.)
Given the facts presented, it is substantially likely that the city council's decisions
regarding mobile home parks in the city of Marina will have a material financial effect on your
client. Accordingly, you will be disqualified from participating in council decisions on the
mobile home parks decision affecting your client. Ultimately, we leave this factor for your
consideration; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. iln ,"
Oglesby (1975) I FFPC Ops. 71.)
Steps Seven and Eight: Does the governmental decision come within any exception to the
confl ict-of-interest rules?
From the facts submitted, there is no indication that either the "public generally" or
"legally required participation" exceptions would be applicable in the present-case. Accordingly,
we have not provided an analysis involving those steps. If you find that additional facts mighl 
- '
indicate that it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality threshold will be met and you riish
further advice on the public generally exception, please feel free to write again.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Nubia I. Lemus
Legal Intem, Legal Division
NIL:jgl

prevent disqualification?

Section 87103(c).) Therefore, you will not be able to participate in any decisions that will have a
